instructor.name: Thomas Ferris, Ph.D.
instructor.office_hours: Ferris' Regular Office Hours: Thursdays, 3:00 – 5:00 PM Central Time (In-person: 4081 ETB, Also on Zoom (in-person get priority): https://tamu.zoom.us/my/thomasferris); "Happy" Office Hours: Thursdays, 6:00 PM, Zoom only: https://tamu.zoom.us/my/thomasferris; Office Hours also available by appointment (tferris@tamu.edu)

course_metadata.meeting_times: 3:00 PM – 3:50 PM, Monday, Wednesday, and Friday

chunks.title: *Disclaimer*
chunks.content:
This syllabus will be reviewed to flag any topics taught which might be deemed controversial by the newly-established State of Texas Ombudsman's Officers, appointed by Governor Greg Abbott.

Although there are no planned lecture topics that appear to fit into this ill-defined category, Dr. Ferris often feels it is appropriate and necessary to discuss current events and to analyze how problematic systems develop from a Cognitive Systems Engineering perspective. Because many salient problems today touch on some of these controversial topics, it is possible that some course discussion may be unintentionally offensive. I will always do my best to respect and treat all students equally, regardless of political/religious/cultural beliefs, and I expect each student in my class to extend that respect to their classmates (and to me). During class discussions, new ideas are welcome and will be discussed critically in the context of some of the methodologies taught in this course. As always, you are welcome to come to me in confidence to share your feelings on these topics, and I will do my best to be objective and endorse critical thinking exercises so you can find satisfying answers.

Effective January 1, 2026, State of Texas Senate Bill 37 mandates a reporting portal for any state of Texas employees who endorse controversial ideologies. These reports are reviewed by the Ombudsman's office, and steps can then be taken to force changes to the course topics and/or dismissal of the associated state employees. Legitimate reports/complaints can be submitted here: https://www.studentsfirst.texas.gov/ombudsman-complaint-portal/

chunks.title: *Personal Statement*
chunks.content: I believe strongly that Texas students suffer, as does the academic reputation of Texas institutions, when politicians smother academic freedom and push for censorship of scholarly topics because they appear counter to the worldview of the politicians in power. – Tom Ferris

chunks.title: Text:
chunks.content: There is no required textbook. All course materials including readings, lecture slides, and lecture videos will be hosted on Canvas: https://canvas.tamu.edu/courses/436893

chunks.title: Scope and Objectives:
chunks.content: This course will provide an overview of the field of Cognitive Systems Engineering (CSE), which concerns how humans and technologies interact to perform cognitive work in sociotechnical systems. It will introduce underlying theory and methods in CSE that are applicable to the design of processes, tools, and technologies to support cognitive functions such as communication, planning, decision-making, and problem-solving in complex work domains. It will analyze how artifacts, displays, social interactions, and factors such as stress, time pressure, competing demands, and uncertainty affect performance of cognitive systems. Examples of current trends in CSE research will be discussed. Task analysis and human-centered design methods will be applied to a group project throughout the semester.

chunks.title: Grade Determination:
chunks.content:
20% Reading responses and Discussion board
50% Exams: midterm (25%) and final exam (25%)
30% Semester project deliverables

Grades will be calculated on the basis of total points earned. If deemed necessary, the grade thresholds may be curved based on class average and may be lower than the following standard (out of a total of 100 percentage points).
A: 90-100%
B: 80-89%
C: 70-79%
D: 60-69%
F: 59% and lower

chunks.title: Reading Responses:
chunks.content:
With few exceptions, there will be an assigned reading related to the lecture material for each class. The readings will be posted on Canvas. As a way to jump-start class discussion, you will need to submit a reading response (RR) for each assigned reading via Canvas by 12:00 PM (noon) on the day BEFORE the associated lecture, unless otherwise specified. These responses must include header material (name, RR, date) and two clearly distinguished sections (I suggest using the following headers):

1) Discussion point(s), which should be at least one of the following:
A) An insightful question about the reading. Time permitting, I will address as many of these questions as I can in lecture, and classmates will have a chance to respond as well.
B) A comment about how the reading relates to other course concepts or to an example/anecdote from your own life experience.
2) Summary of the reading, in your own words (copying verbatim from the reading or other sources is considered plagiarism and will be treated according to the Academic Misconduct Policy). This summary is primarily meant to serve as a study aid (e.g., when reviewing for exams), and so should be organized in a way that best helps you. At a minimum, include the main messages of the reading as well as other relevant and/or interesting concepts discussed.

An RR template and grading rubric will be posted in Canvas. The lengths of each of the Discussion points and Summary sections can be flexible, but combined should be approximately 1 page in length. The reading response exercises are designed to support assimilation and retention of the course material, and also to identify emphases for me to cover in lecture. We will also respond to each other's discussion points online, and continue discussions in lecture.

chunks.title: Discussion Board:
chunks.content: As a means to jumpstart class discussion, the discussion points from the Reading Responses should be posted on Discussion Boards (DBs) in Canvas. In addition to posting discussion points/questions, students will also read and respond to your classmates' points/questions. The specific format for this activity is TBD (dependent on the course enrollment size) and will be communicated in the first week of class. DB responses will be assessed and associated with points in the “Reading response and Discussion board" grade category.

chunks.title: Late Policy:
chunks.content: Items that are submitted after the posted due date/time will not receive any credit. Exceptions can be made in accordance with university rules (https://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07/). In these cases, documentation of the circumstances will be required.

chunks.title: Exams:
chunks.content: There will be two open-note, take-home written exams: a midterm which is tentatively scheduled for Week 8 (25% of final grade), and a comprehensive final exam due on or before May 4th (25% of final grade). Each exam will consist primarily of short answer and/or essay questions that emphasize application of the material discussed in lecture and assigned readings. Exam dates can be flexible in cases of need but must be communicated to Dr. Ferris prior to the exam's scheduled week.

chunks.title: Semester Project:
chunks.content:
Teams of students will collaborate on a project analyzing the cognitive work performed in a sociotechnical system of their choice. This project will take part in 3 phases, each with its own deliverables. Note that the 3-phase plan below is tentative and may change due to the availability of resources (any changes will be communicated in class and via announcements).

*   Phase 0: Propose sociotechnical systems of interest for this project. Dr. Ferris will provide feedback and guide the selection.
    *   Deliverable: Project proposal (5% of project grade)
*   Phase 1: Students will define/model the sociotechnical system and perform a front end analysis on that system. This will serve to inform the in situ data collection and guide the task analysis in Phase 2.
    *   Deliverable: Front end analysis report (25% of project grade)
*   Phase 2: Students will apply observational and task analysis techniques to formally describe system tasks/functions that are critical to the main outputs of interest in the sociotechnical system. In addition to a task analysis description, the report deliverable will include identification of subtasks that are problematic or could likely be improved with different technologies, training, or procedures.
    *   Deliverable: Task analysis description and report (30% of project grade)
*   Phase 3: Students will design a "solution” to improve the effectiveness of the analyzed system according to shortcomings that were identified in Phase 2. This solution may take several forms, for example, it may involve the development of technology to aid humans in the system (e.g., software tool, external memory aid, information visualization), a procedural change, or a training method. An additional requirement may be for the solution to be evaluated using techniques discussed in class (e.g., usability study, focus groups/interviews/surveys, mini-experiments). Project groups will work with Dr. Ferris to determine the appropriate scope for this activity.
    *   Deliverables: Solution and evaluation report (30% of project grade), in-class presentation (10% of project grade)

chunks.title: Semester Project (continued)
chunks.content: Each team member is expected to contribute to each phase of the project and each deliverable. Students will be required to submit peer evaluation forms for the members of their team, and individual grades will be adjusted up/down based on the combined evaluation forms from all team members. Students are strongly encouraged to contact Dr. Ferris early on if there are any problems with/between team members that require attention.

chunks.title: Re-grading Policy:
chunks.content: Students have 1 week after grades are released for an assignment or exam to submit a re-grade request in writing. This request must not exceed 1 page (11 point font, single spacing), and must clearly indicate all the relevant problem(s) and justification for why you think re-grading is warranted. Note that a requested re-grade may result in further point deductions if new errors are discovered.

chunks.title: Plagiarism Policy
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. Al text generators (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to:
* Creating or revising drafts
* Editing your work
* Reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.

Such use of Al text generators in this manner could be considered plagiarism and cheating according to Student Rule 20. More information may also be found at https://aggiehonor.tamu.edu or you may contact your instructor if you have questions.
